title: Energy-Weighted Site Percolation in Two Dimensions
abstract: We study a generalization of two-dimensional site percolation by assigning an energy cost $\varepsilon$ to bonds between nearest-neighbor occupied sites. This leads to a competition between entropy-driven cluster growth and energetic suppression (or enhancement) of connectivity. Varying $\varepsilon$ continuously interpolates between dense ferromagnetic-like clusters, ordinary classical percolation, and a dilute regime of minimally connected isolated clusters. Using Monte Carlo simulations and real-space renormalization-group (RG) methods, we show that bond energy shifts the percolation threshold smoothly. We define an energy-weighted correlation length that remains finite at the classical site occupation threshold ($p_c(\varepsilon=0)$) and shrinks with increasing $\varepsilon$, capturing the energetic suppression of large-scale connectivity. The cluster size distribution exhibits an energy-dependent cutoff that drives the transition from percolation-like clusters to isolated clusters. A real-space RG with Kadanoff block recursions reveals a systematic evolution of the correlation-length exponent $ν$ from $ν=1/2$ (dense clusters) to $ν=4/3$ (classical percolation), approaching $ν=1$ (minimally connected isolated clusters), in agreement with Coulomb-gas predictions for loop models where bond energy renormalizes loop fugacity. For large values of \(\varepsilon\) (isotropic case), the suppression of nearest-neighbor bonds results in the emergence of antiferromagnetic sub-lattice ordering at high densities. Additionally, anisotropic bond energies lead to directionally selective cluster growth. Finally, we also discuss a lattice gas RG approach and scenarios where bond energy is renormalized across different scales.
summary: This paper generalizes 2D site percolation by introducing a bond energy cost, demonstrating that this parameter can continuously tune the system between different universality classes. By using renormalization group and Monte Carlo methods, the authors show how connectivity and critical exponents evolve as a function of the energetic cost.
main_problem: The study investigates how assigning an energy cost to bonds between nearest-neighbor occupied sites affects the percolation threshold, critical exponents, and universality classes in 2D site percolation.
main_result: The bond energy epsilon acts as a tuning parameter that shifts the percolation threshold and drives a continuous evolution of the correlation length exponent nu from 1/2 to 4/3 to 1, effectively interpolating between different universality classes.
method: The authors employ Monte Carlo simulations in a grand-canonical ensemble, real-space renormalization group (RG) methods using Kadanoff block recursions, and a Coulomb-gas formulation to interpret scaling behavior.
model_or_system: An energy-weighted site percolation model on a 2D square lattice where each bond between nearest-neighbor occupied sites incurs an energy cost epsilon, creating a competition between entropy and energetic suppression.
key_observables: Percolation threshold (pc), correlation length (xi) and its exponent (nu), cluster size distribution, and susceptibility (chi).
important_parameters: Site occupation probability (p), bond energy cost (epsilon), and anisotropic energy components (epsilon_x, epsilon_y).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of real-space RG approximations and uses specific renormalization rules (e.g., R0, R2) for block-based calculations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate lattice configurations with energy costs, phase diagrams of cluster configurations, RG flow diagrams around fixed points, and scaling collapses of wrapping probability and density.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the energy-weighted model, details the Monte Carlo and RG methodologies, presents results for isotropic and anisotropic cases, provides a Coulomb-gas interpretation, and concludes with a discussion on lattice gas RG approaches.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A 4 × 4 square lattice with 12 occupied (blue) and 4 unoccupied (red) sites. Bonds between nearest-neighbor occupied sites are shown in black and have an energy cost εx for bonds along horizontal x direction, and energy cost εy for bonds along vertical y direction. The connected occupied sites form a cluster. In this diagram, there is only one cluster containing 12 occupied sites and 16 bonds connecting nearest- neighbor occupied sites.

FIG. 2. The left plot (a) illustrates the variation in cluster configurations as a function of site-occupation probability p and energy cost ε, and the right plot (b) illustrates the variation in cluster configurations for different values of site occupation probability p and energy cost εx for bonds along the direction of x axis and εy for bonds along the direction of y axis. The computation is performed on a square grid with a total of N = 400 sites. The colors indicate the occupancy variable nij, with nij = 1 indicating that a particular site is occupied (yellow), and nij = 0 indicating that a particular site is not occupied (blue).

FIG. 3. Phase diagram indicating percolating and non- percolating phase separated by critical curve pc(ε), in the ε −p plane. The phase diagram is obtained using numerical Monte Carlo simulation on a square lattice with dimensions N = 40000, as detailed in Sec. III. The blue colored region indicates the non-percolating phase, while the red colored re- gion indicates the percolating phase.
FIG. 4. The plot illustrates the variation in the mean density of occupied sites, defined as ρ = Nocc

FIG. 5. The plot illustrates the density of occupied sites, de- noted as ρ(pc(ε)), which is evaluated at the critical site occu- pation probability pc(ε) for various values of the bond energy cost ε. The data for pc(ε) is also presented in Fig. 3. Ad- ditionally, the corresponding mean density of occupied sites, ρ(pc(ε), ε), is computed for a system size of N = 400.
